docs.title: Home
docs.text: This page is still under construction

docs.title: About
docs.text:
This is a library for free math education, it includes material from a bunch of courses given in all the major israeli universities.

This project is created by MkDocs and uses the Material for MkDocs theme, all the documentation from there can be applied for this project.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
If you want to contribute to this project and help math be as accessible as possible you can do so by creating a pull request on any of the pages in the project (and even create your own!), or you can open a new issue on the github page of this project.

To contribute directly you need to clone the project using,

git clone https://github.com/yeahBOYYYYY/math-library/

From that alone you now can now create new .md files under the folder docs/ to be displayed in the site, but note that you won't be able to locally view your changes.

docs.title: Setting Up the Project Locally
docs.text:
For starters you need to have Python 3.x installed on your machine since all the libraries involved in the project are downloaded with pip.

After setting up python, you need to install MkDocs and the Material theme using,

pip install mkdocs
pip install mkdocs-material

Then you'll need to install the plugins used to deploy this site,

pip install mkdocs-git-authors-plugin
pip install mkdocs-git-revision-date-localized-plugin

Now you can run the project locally by running the following command from the folder with the mkdocs.yml file,

mkdocs serve

This will open a new local server on your machine (default is port 8000), and will show in the console the URL for the local site, like so,

INFO    -  [16:32:38] Serving on http://127.0.0.1:8000/math-library/

And from now on all the changes you make locally will show up live at this URL.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: The structure of this site allows for either .md file types or .html to be viewed, but be aware that all html code will be displayed as is in the final site while markdown will be parsed by Python-Markdown.

docs.title: Admonitions
docs.text:
While Python-Markdown supports the full GitHub Markdown flavour, it also provides extra functionality, the main one being "admonitions",

!!! quote "This is an admonition"

    Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

    ??? tip "This is a nested opening admonition"

        Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

And visually,

This is an admonition

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

This is a nested opening admonition

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

For extended documentation on this feature visit the Material for MkDocs documentation.

docs.title: Custom Admonitions
docs.text: This project defines extra admonitions for math use, in LaTex you can use environments like "Proof" or "Example", but this doesn't have a markdown counterpart, hence we use admonitions to display those environments.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
This uses the "def" keyword,

Definition: This is a definition block

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

docs.title: Theorem
docs.text:
This uses the "thm" keyword,

Theorem: This is a theorem block

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nulla et euismod nulla. Curabitur feugiat, tortor non consequat finibus, justo purus auctor massa, nec semper lorem quam in massa.

To give extensive information about a "Theorem", "Lemma" or "Claim" we use the following syntax for the title,

_Theorem_ [ < list of last names and year of authors > ]: < name of the theorem >

For example,

Theorem [Jones-Brown 2025, Smith 2026]: Three Broomstick Theorem

And hyperlinks to give the authors contribution are encouraged!

docs.title: Logic
docs.text:
Mathematical logic is a branch of mathematics that deals with the expression of simple and complex mathematical statements. It allows for the removal of the multiplicity of meanings that exists in human languages by establishing precise rules for presenting different statements in a way that can be understood in only one way. Logic also deals with systems of rules for drawing valid conclusions from data or assumptions in our possession.

Example: Uncertainty in Language

When you are in a restaurant and ask for a Coke, the waitress asks, "Would you like regular or zero?" Of course, the word or in this case refers to the choice between one of the two and not both, on the other hand, when asked, "Are you hungry or thirsty?" You can mean either one of the two or perhaps both at the same time, meaning that the word or has a double meaning depending on the context.

Logic began its journey in ancient Greece as a branch of philosophy, but following many significant developments in its formal directions, it is now also considered a branch of mathematics. Logic serves as the central theoretical basis for computer science. It is necessary, for example, when we want to "talk" to a machine (such as a computer), because communication with a computer must be very precise and unambiguous, and must comply with predetermined logical rules. Developing logical thinking is also necessary for dealing with various and diverse problems, especially in the fields of science and technology, which require precise and orderly analysis of processes and ideas. In addition, the ability to justify arguments accurately while avoiding logical fallacies that would harm the validity of conclusions is a central tool in mathematics and computer science.

Note: Base Assumptions

All logic in this chapter is based on a system of axioms called \(\mathrm{ZF}\), beyond this chapter we will always use the axiom system \(\mathrm{ZFC}\) (we assume the axiom of choice in this course).

docs.title: Propositional Calculus
docs.text:
This type of logic deals with sentences, a mathematical object that can be either true or false, and the relations between those sentences.

Definition: Formalization

The transformation of a literal verse into a language of mathematical forms.

Example

We'll formalize the sentence "Today is not Tuesday, today is Monday and tomorrow will be Tuesday", to do that we will define three sentences,

\[\begin{aligned} a & =\text{"Today is Tuesday"} & b & =\text{"Today is Monday"} & c & =\text{"Tomorrow is Tuesday"} \end{aligned}\]

Then our original sentence takes the following form, "(not \(a\)) and (\(b\) and \(c\))".

Definition: Atomic Sentence

A self-contained sentence that can be either \(\true\) or \(\false\).

Example

"It is raining outside" Is an atomic sentence because it can be \(\true\) or \(\false\) and is not composed of sub-sentences that can be \(\true\) or \(\false\).

docs.title: Logical Connectives
docs.text:
Until now we talked about atomic sentence in mathematics but we still can't express complex sentences, for that we need connectives between atomic sentences.

Definition: Negation Connective

Given a sentence \(A\) we express "not \(A\)" symbolically as "\(\neg A\)".

Note

It is also common to use the notation "\(\sim A\)" or "\(\overline{A}\)" for negation, but we will use only "\(\neg A\)".

Definition: Conjunction Connective

Given two sentences \(A\) and \(B\) we express "\(A\) and \(B\)" symbolically as "\(A \land B\)".

Definition: Disjunction Connective

Given two sentences \(A\) and \(B\) we express "\(A\) or \(B\)" symbolically as "\(A \lor B\)".

Definition: Implication Connective

Given two sentences \(A\) and \(B\) we express "if \(A\) then \(B\)" symbolically as "\(A \implies B\)", and we call \(A\) the "Prefix" and \(B\) the "Suffix" of the sentence.

docs.title: Sentence
docs.text:
Now after defining the atomic sentences and the connectives we can define what a sentence is formally,

Definition: Sentence

A claim that is composed by atomic sentences and connectives between them.

Example

The phrase "Today is Tuesday" is a sentence, while "What is the time?", "Close the door!" and "1 + 1" are not.

Example

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be atomic sentences, then the following phrases are also sentences,

  • \(A \implies B\)
  • \(\left(A\lor B\right)\land\left(C\lor B\right)\)
  • \(\left(\left(A\lor B\right)\implies\neg A\right)\implies C\)

Note

Phrases of the form,

  • \(A\implies\)
  • \(A\land\lor B\)
  • \(A\land B\implies C\)
  • \(A \neg\)

Which are phrases without mathematical meaning or with dual meaning are not considered as sentence in propositional calculus, and such phrases are called "abuse of notation".

docs.title: Values of Sentences
docs.text:
Now that we have sentences in our mathematical language we want to give them meaning, for that we have to talk about truth and falseness of sentences.

Definition: Truth Assignment

Let \(A\) be an atomic sentence, then we assign to \(A\) a value of either \( rue\) or \( alse\). Moreover we'll use the notation \(V\left(A ight)\) for the truth assignment of \(A\).

Note

In our logic system a sentence is either \( rue\) or \( alse\) but never both.

And mathematically, for all atomic sentences \(A\) the following holds,

\[\left(\left(V\left(A ight)= rue ight)\land\left(V\left(A ight) e alse ight) ight)\lor\left(\left(V\left(A ight)= alse ight)\land\left(V\left(A ight) e rue ight) ight)\]

From the last note we can infer the rule "reductio ad absurdum", which means in English "proof by contradiction", to prove that a statement \(\varphi\) is true we can assume that it's false and show that it implies a contradiction, hence because we have only two options for the truthiness of \(\varphi\) we can deduce that \(\varphi\) must be true!

Example

This are a few sentences and their truth assignments,

  • \(V\left(1<3 ight) = rue\)
  • \(V\left(1+ 1 = 3 ight) = alse\)
Exercise

Formalize and determine if the following sentence is true, false or not a sentence, "Today is Tuesday and Tomorrow will also be Tuesday".

Solution

We will define two sentences,

\[ egin{aligned} A & = ext{"Today is Tuesday"} & B & = ext{"Tomorrow is Tuesday"} \end{aligned}\]

Then our original sentence is \(A \land B\), but we also note that only of them can be true by our understanding of the week days, hence we can that

\[V\left(A\land B ight) = alse\]

Claim

Let \(A_1, \ldots, A_n\) be atomic sentences then there are overall \(2^n\) possible assignments of all the atomic sentences to truth values.

Proof

Each atomic sentence \(A_i\) can be assigned to either \( rue\) or \( alse\), therefore each \(A_i\) has two options for it's assignment, and the atomic sentences don't have any connection between then (since they are atomic, and therefore the choice of their values is arbitrary). Hence overall there are

\[\underbrace{2\cdot\ldots\cdot2}_{n}=2^{n}\]

assignments of truth values to \(A_1, \ldots, A_n\).

Now because we know that there are only finite number of assignments possible for a collection of atomic sentences, we can create a table that summarizes the truthfulness of each logical connective!

Definition: Truth Table

A table that summarizes the truth value of a complex sentence given a change in the truth assignment of each of the atomic sentences that he's composed of.

Definition: Truth Tables for the Logical Connectives

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be atomic sentences then,

Definition: Truth Table for the Negation Connective \(V\left(A ight)\) \(V\left( eg A ight)\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) Definition: Truth Table for the Conjunction Connective \(V\left(A ight)\) \(V\left(B ight)\) \(V\left(A\land B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) Definition: Truth Table for the Disjunction Connective \(V\left(A ight)\) \(V\left(B ight)\) \(V\left(A\lor B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) Definition: Truth Table for the Implication Connective \(V\left(A ight)\) \(V\left(B ight)\) \(V\left(A\implies B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\)

Wait but how did the two last entries in the implication table got assigned to true? Well we can think it through with a simpler example,

Example: Falseness Implies All

Imagine a parent tells their child "If it rains, then I will take you to the movies.", then four options may occur,

  • It rains and they go to the movies: Promise kept (True).
  • It rains and they don't go: Promise broken (False).
  • It does not rain and they go anyway: The parent didn't break their promise. The promise only applied "if it rains." (True).
  • It does not rain and they don't go: Again, the parent didn't break their promise. (True).

This is actually a bigger phenomena in logic that we can note and formalize!

Note: Principle of Explosion

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be atomic sentences then,

\[\left(V\left(A ight) = alse ight) \implies \left(V\left(A \implies B ight) = rue ight)\]

Now given the truth assignment of the connectives between atomic sentences we can finally talk about the truth assignment of all sentences, this is done by noticing that all sentences by definition are compose from atomic sentences and connectives between them.

Example

Given that \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) are atomic sentences, we want to create a truth table for the following sentence,

\[\left(\left(A\land B ight)\implies C ight)\implies\left(\left( eg A ight)\lor C ight)\] \(A\) \(B\) \(C\) \(A\land B\) \(\left(A\land B ight)\implies C\) \( eg A\) \(\left( eg A ight)\lor C\) \(\left(\left(A\land B ight)\implies C ight)\implies\left(\left( eg A ight)\lor C ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\)

* If the math in the table shows tiny please right click on it and zoom.

Exercise: Deducing From Truth Tables

Try to deduce the truth assignment from the following given data,

  1. Given that \(A \lor \left( eg B ight)\) is a false sentence, what can we deduce?
    1. \(A\) is true, \(B\) is true.
    2. \(A\) is true, \(B\) is false.
    3. \(A\) can't be determined, \(B\) is true.
    4. \(A\) is false, \(B\) is true.
    5. \(A\) is false, \(B\) can't be determined.
  2. Given that \(A\) and \(B\) are both false, what can we infer about \(\left(A\implies B ight) \land \left(B\implies A ight)\)?
    1. It's a true sentence.
    2. It's a false sentence.
    3. It can't be determined.
Solution
  1. The solution is answer "d", by the truth table of the disjunction connective we know that for the result to be false both the sentenced being or-ed must be false themselves, hence \(A\) is false, and \( eg B\) is false, then by the truth table of the negation connective we know that for \( eg B\) to be false it must follow that \(B\) is true.
  2. The solution is answer "a". First of all we need to look at \(A \implies B\) and \(B \implies A\) separately to infer about the whole complex sentence, since we know that \(A\) is false we know by the truth table of the implication connective that \(A \implies B\) is true, likewise because \(B\) is false we know by the truth table of the implication connective that \(B \implies A\) is true. Now, we can see that by the truth table of the conjunction connective that \(\left(A\implies B ight) \land \left(B\implies A ight)\) must be true.

docs.title: Equivalence of Sentences
docs.text:
After talking about sentences and their truth value, we want to be able to distinguish different logic sentences, for example we know that "Today is Monday and tomorrow is Tuesday" is equivalent to saying "Tomorrow is Tuesday and today is Monday", but we want to give this a formal definition,

Definition: Sentence Equivalence

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences such that, for every truth assignment of the atomic sentences the following holds,

\[V\left(A ight) = V\left(B ight)\]

then we call \(A\) and \(B\) equivalent sentences, and use the notation \(A \equiv B\).

Note

Given two sentences \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\), if the truth table of \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) is the same then we know that they are equivalent, this is because by definition the truth table shows us the result of the truth assignment for \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) under every truth assignment to the atomic sentence that makes them.

Now that we know how to prove equivalence of sentences we want to show some commonly used results that will be very important for your entire math journey.

First of all we show that conjunction and disjunction are commutative, this is a neat trick to use when needed!

Claim: Conjunction is Commutative

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences then,

\[A\land B\equiv B\land A\] Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(A \land B\) \(B \land A\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) Exercise: Disjunction is Commutative

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences, show that the following holds,

\[A\lor B\equiv B\lor A\] Solution

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(A \lor B\) \(B \lor A\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\)

Secondly we show that conjunction and disjunction are associative, this is very useful for reducing complex sentences by shuffling the parentheses around them.

Claim: Conjunction is Associative

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be sentences then,

\[A\land\left(B\land C ight)\equiv\left(A\land B ight)\land C\] Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(C\) \(A\land\left(B\land C ight)\) \(\left(A\land B ight)\land C\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) Exercise: Disjunction is Associative

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences, show that the following holds,

\[A\lor\left(B\lor C ight)\equiv\left(A\lor B ight)\lor C\] Solution

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(C\) \(A\lor\left(B\lor C ight)\) \(\left(A\lor B ight)\lor C\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\)

Thirdly we show that conjunction and disjunction are distributive over one another, this is also very useful for reducing complex sentences by being able to pull out conjunctions and disjunctions.

Claim: Conjunction is Distributive Over Disjunction

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be sentences then,

\[A\land\left(B\lor C ight)\equiv\left(A\land B ight)\lor\left(A\land C ight)\] Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(C\) \(A\land\left(B\lor C ight)\) \(\left(A\land B ight)\lor\left(A\land C ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) Exercise: Disjunction is Distributive Over Conjunction

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences, show that the following holds,

\[A\lor\left(B\land C ight)\equiv\left(A\lor B ight)\land\left(A\lor C ight)\] Solution

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(C\) \(A\lor\left(B\land C ight)\) \(\left(A\lor B ight)\land\left(A\lor C ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\)

Now we still want to show some useful equivalences to use.

Claim: Negation is an Involution

Let \(A\) be a sentence then,

\[ eg\left( eg A ight)\equiv A\] Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \( eg A\) \( eg \left( eg A ight)\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\)

Claim: Contraposition

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences then,

\[A\implies B\equiv\left( eg B ight)\implies\left( eg A ight)\] Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

\(A\) \(B\) \(A\implies B\) \(\left( eg B ight)\implies\left( eg A ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) Exercise

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences, show that each of the following holds,

  1. \(A\implies B\equiv\left( eg A ight)\lor B\).
  2. \( eg\left(A\implies B ight)\equiv A\land\left( eg B ight)\).
Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

1.

\(A\) \(B\) \(A\implies B\) \(\left( eg A ight)\lor B\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\)

2.

\(A\) \(B\) \( eg\left(A\implies B ight)\) \(A\land\left( eg B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\)

Claim: De Morgan's laws

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences then,

  1. \( eg\left(A\land B ight)\equiv\left( eg A ight)\lor\left( eg B ight)\).
  2. \( eg\left(A\lor B ight)\equiv\left( eg A ight)\land\left( eg B ight)\).
Proof

We will show that the columns in the truth table match,

1.

\(A\) \(B\) \( eg\left(A\land B ight)\) \(\left( eg A ight)\lor\left( eg B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\)

2.

\(A\) \(B\) \( eg\left(A\lor B ight)\) \(\left( eg A ight)\land\left( eg B ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( alse\) \( rue\) \( rue\)

Given two sentences that imply each other, we know that this means that they are both true or false together, hence this is the strongest relationship two sentences can have.

Definition: If and Only If

Let \(A\) and \(B\) be sentences such that,

\[\left(A\implies B ight)\land\left(B\implies A ight)\]

Then we use the notation \(A\iff B\) and say that "\(A\) if and only if \(B\)", or abbreviated to "\(A\) iff \(B\)".

Definition: Tautology

Let \(A\) be a sentence such that, for every truth assignment of the atomic sentences the following holds,

\[V\left(A ight) = rue\]

then we call \(A\) a tautology.

Example

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be sentences, then the following is a tautology,

\[\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight)\implies\left(A\implies C ight)\] Proof

  • If we assume that

    \[V\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight) = alse\]

    then

    \[V\left(\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight)\implies\left(A\implies C ight) ight) = rue\]

    as we wanted.

  • Else we assume that

    \[V\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight) = rue\]

    then from the truth table of conjunction we get that,

    \[\left(V\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight) = rue ight) \land \left(V\left(B ight) = rue ight)\]

    now let's split to cases based on the truth assignment of \(A\),

    • If we assume that \(V\left(A ight) = alse\), then \(V\left(A\implies C ight)= rue\), and hence

      \[V\left(\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight)\implies\left(A\implies C ight) ight) = rue\]

      as we wanted.

    • Else we assume that \(V\left(A ight) = rue\), then \(V\left(B\implies C ight) = rue\), and hence because we assume that \(V\left(B ight)= rue\) we can infer that \(V\left(C ight)= rue\), from that we get that \(V\left(A\implies C ight)= rue\), and all in all we get that,

      \[V\left(\left(\left(A\implies\left(B\implies C ight) ight)\land B ight)\implies\left(A\implies C ight) ight) = rue\]

      as we wanted.

Definition: Contradiction

Let \(A\) be a sentence such that, for every truth assignment of the atomic sentences the following holds,

\[V\left(A ight) = alse\]

then we call \(A\) a contradiction.

Exercise: Tautologies and Contradictions are Opposites

Let \(A\) be a sentence then,

\[\left(A ext{ is a tautology} ight)\iff \left( eg A ext{ is a contradiction} ight)\]

Claim

Let \(A\) be a sentence, then the following are tautologies

  1. \(A \implies A\)
  2. \(A \lor \left( eg A ight)\)
Proof

We need to create the truth table for those sentences and show that they're always true.

1.

\(A\) \(A \implies A\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\)

2.

\(A\) \(A \lor \left( eg A ight)\) \( rue\) \( rue\) \( alse\) \( rue\)

Definition: Semantically Entailment Sentence

Let \(A_1,\ldots , A_n\) be sentences and let \(B\) be a sentence, such that the following holds,

\[\left( ext{For all $i$ in range $1$ to $n$ we have} V\left(A_i ight) = rue ight) \implies V\left(B ight) = rue\]

then we call \(B\) a semantically entailment sentence in \(A_1,\ldots , A_n\).

Example

Let \(\varphi\) and \(\phi\) be sentences, and define the following,

\[ egin{aligned} A_{1}&= eg\left(\varphi\implies\psi ight)&A_{2}&=\psi&B&=\varphi \end{aligned}\]

then \(B\) is a semantically entailment sentence in \(A_1, A_2\).

Proof

Assume by contradiction that

\[ egin{aligned} V\left(A_{1} ight)&= rue&V\left(A_{2} ight)&= rue \end{aligned}\]

in particular we get that,

\[V\left( eg\left(\varphi\implies\psi ight) ight)= rue\]

and hence,

\[V\left(\varphi\implies\psi ight)= alse\]

but as we assumed it is also true that \(V\left(\psi ight)= rue\), meaning it's impossible that \(V\left(\varphi ight)= rue\) because if that was true then we would have gotten that,

\[V\left(\varphi\implies\psi ight)= rue\]

Hence we get that \(V\left(\varphi ight)= alse\) and particularly \(V\left( eg\varphi ight)= rue\), meaning that \(V\left(B ight)= rue\), which is exactly the definition that, \(B\) is a semantically entailment sentence in \(A_1, A_2\), as we wanted.

Exercise

Let \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) be sentences, determine wether the sentence \(B\implies C\) is a semantically entailment sentence in both \(A\implies B\) and \(A\implies C\).

docs.title: Predicate Calculus
docs.text:
After dealing with sentences in propositional calculus we want to deal with sentences that contain variables and quantifiers on those variables.

Definition: Predicate

A predicate in \(n\) variables is a proposition in \(n\) terms.

The proposition "There exists \(x\) such that \(x^2 = -1\)" is a predicate in one variable, but on which domain does this defined? Does it even have meaning for all mathematical object? Likewise the proposition "For all \(x, y\) holds that \(x > y\)" is a predicate in two variables, but again we don't know that is the domain of \(x, y\), are they numbers? Maybe they're some arbitrary objects?
